search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Heatwave rising: Will it lead to higher UV radiation, and should you be worried?
As temperatures shatter records across the country, an invisible hazard is intensifying overhead and most people have no idea how serious the risk is.
Kerala's health authorities issued advisories just weeks ago, warning residents about extremely high ultraviolet (UV) radiation levels being recorded across the state, even on days when the sky appeared overcast.
The warnings came as temperatures in several parts of Kerala climbed well above seasonal norms, with the broader Indian subcontinent in the grip of one of its most intense pre-monsoon heat waves in years.
On Monday, May 18, UV radiation levels across major Indian cities painted a clear picture of how intense the summer is.
According to AQI.in, New Delhi recorded UV radiation levels of 7.2 at around 3:15 pm on Monday, May 18. Mumbai recorded "very high" levels of 8.5; Thiruvananthapuram came in at a "high" 6.9.
But while most heatwave advisories focus on dehydration and heat stroke, the invisible energy beaming down from the Sun is a separate, compounding threat that often goes unacknowledged.
WHAT IS UV RADIATION?
UV radiation is a form of energy emitted by the Sun that is invisible to the naked eye.
The radiation sits just beyond the violet end of the visible light spectrum and is broadly divided into three types: UVA, UVB, and UVC.
Of the three, UVC is almost entirely absorbed by the Earth's atmosphere before it reaches us. UVA and UVB, however, reach the surface and both cause harm.
And out of the two types reaching us, UVB is the more immediately dangerous of the two.
UVB is responsible for sunburn, direct DNA damage in skin cells, and is the primary driver of skin cancer.
UVA, on the other hand, penetrates deeper into the skin, accelerates ageing, and contributes to long-term cellular damage.
Critically, UV radiation does not feel like anything. You cannot sense it the way you sense heat, and by the time your skin turns red or feels tender, the damage has already happened.
The UV Index, which is the internationally used scale to measure radiation intensity, runs from 0 to 11+.
Above 8 is classified as very high, and above 11 is classified as extreme.
During peak summer months, large parts of India regularly record UV Index values between 10 and 13, with southern states like Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and coastal Andhra Pradesh among the worst affected due to their proximity to the equator and the near-vertical angle at which the sun strikes the ground.
In other words, India is often a receptor of the harmful radiation that arrives abundantly during summer which is already growing longer and more intense.
WHY IS INDIA AT RISK?
India sits almost entirely within the tropics, meaning the Sun is overhead or very close to it, for much of the year.
This geometry matters enormously when it comes to what regions face impacts and which ones stay relatively safe.
When the sun is directly overhead, UV radiation travels a shorter path through the atmosphere, losing less energy before it hits the ground. Compare this to a country in Northern Europe where the Sun sits low on the horizon and radiation passes through a far thicker slice of atmosphere.
Additionally, India's monsoon-adjacent seasons create a false sense of safety.
On hazy or partly cloudy days, UV levels can still be dangerously high, as clouds block visible light and heat more effectively than they block UV.
A cool, overcast morning in May can carry UV Index readings of 9 or above, exposing people who step outside without protection under the mistaken belief that it is safe.
India also has a large outdoor working population that includes farmers, construction workers, street vendors, rickshaw pullers, and daily wage labourers, who spend hours in direct sunlight with little to no protection.
This population bears a disproportionately high burden of UV-related health risk, yet is the least aware of it.
HOW HARMFUL IS UV RADIATION?
The immediate effects of UV overexposure are commonly experienced. They include sunburn, redness, and peeling skin.
But the longer-term consequences are far more serious and concerning.
Repeated UV exposure damages the DNA inside skin cells over time, increasing the risk of skin cancers. It also causes cataracts, a clouding of the eye's lens that is already among the leading causes of blindness in India.
The World Health Organisation (WHO) estimates that up to 20% of cataract cases globally are caused by UV exposure.
Beyond the skin and eyes, UV radiation suppresses the immune system, reducing the body's ability to fight off infections at the very moment it is already under stress from extreme heat.

title: Heatwaves Drive Spike In Illnesses As Doctors Warn Of Dehydration And Heatstroke - BW Healthcare
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
The recent surge in temperature is already showing up in outpatient departments, where doctors are seeing more patients with heat-related complaints. In the past one to two weeks, OPD visits for such symptoms have reportedly risen by an estimated 20 to 30 percent, especially during peak afternoon hours.
Rising Heat Burden
Dr. Pankaj Soni, Principal Director, Internal Medicine, Max Smart Super Speciality Hospital, Saket, said healthcare providers are witnessing a noticeable increase in heat-related illnesses as temperatures continue to rise. He noted that the most common cases currently being seen include dehydration, heat exhaustion, heat cramps, dizziness, low blood pressure episodes, worsening headaches and migraines, urinary infections linked to reduced fluid intake, and aggravation of chronic respiratory and cardiac conditions.
He further pointed out that gastrointestinal complaints such as nausea and loss of appetite are also becoming more frequent during the heatwave period.
Warning Signs People Ignore
One of the biggest concerns, according to Dr. Soni, is that many people ignore the early warning signs of heat-related illness. He said symptoms such as excessive sweating, intense thirst, weakness, fatigue, muscle cramps, headache, nausea, irritability, dizziness, and dark-colored urine may indicate heat exhaustion and should not be dismissed.
He added that if a person develops confusion, disorientation, very high body temperature, cessation of sweating with hot dry skin, seizures, or loss of consciousness, these could point to heatstroke, which is a life-threatening medical emergency.
Who Faces Higher Risk
Certain groups are more vulnerable during a heatwave. Dr. Soni said elderly individuals face greater risk because their ability to regulate body temperature is reduced and many already live with chronic illnesses. He also noted that infants and young children dehydrate quickly, making them especially susceptible.
Outdoor workers, including traffic personnel, construction laborers, and delivery workers, are at particular risk because of prolonged exposure to direct heat. Pregnant women are also more vulnerable due to the increased physiological stress of pregnancy.
Chronic Illnesses Can Worsen
Patients with chronic conditions such as hypertension, diabetes, and migraine can be more affected during extreme heat. Dr. Soni explained that in people with hypertension, dehydration may cause blood pressure fluctuations, while some medicines such as diuretics can increase risk.
He added that diabetic patients may become dehydrated faster and may also experience disturbances in glucose control. Migraine sufferers, meanwhile, often report that heat acts as a trigger and that headaches worsen during prolonged exposure or fluid depletion.
Existing Conditions May Flare
Heat is not only causing new illness; it is also worsening existing disease. Dr. Soni said patients with heart disease may feel greater strain, respiratory illnesses such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease may flare up, and kidney-related issues can worsen if hydration is poor.
He also noted that in some individuals, heat can precipitate fatigue, sleep disturbances, anxiety, and irritability, adding another layer of stress during extreme weather conditions.
Prevention Can Save Lives
Prevention remains the most effective defence against heat-related illness. Dr. Soni advised people to maintain adequate hydration even before feeling thirsty, avoid direct sun exposure during peak hours from noon to 4 PM, wear light and breathable clothing, and avoid strenuous outdoor activity in extreme heat.
He added that electrolyte replacement may be useful for those who sweat heavily, and that homes and workplaces should be kept cool and well ventilated. Vulnerable individuals should never be left in poorly ventilated spaces or parked vehicles.
When It Becomes an Emergency
A heat-related illness becomes a medical emergency when there are signs of heatstroke or severe dehydration. Dr. Soni said persistent vomiting, altered mental status, fainting, inability to drink fluids, very high fever, seizures, or reduced urine output require immediate medical attention.
Delays in treatment can lead to organ damage and may be fatal, making early response critical.With heatwaves becoming more intense and frequent, expert says public awareness is essential. Recognising early symptoms, protecting vulnerable groups, and seeking timely medical help can prevent serious complications and save lives.
